hub_subtitle: Inglês executivo para entrevistas nos EUA e apresentações de alto impacto

lesson.menu_title: Modal Verbs for Executive Authority -- Certain When You Can, Careful When You Must
lesson.menu_desc: Control the force of every sentence with can, should, must, may, and might in an AI safety briefing -- 34 slides
lesson.subtitle: Lesson 6 -- Modal Verbs for Executive Authority
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Lucia Nishiyama Serra | Lesson 6 -- Modal Verbs for Executive Authority

vocab.title: Match each word to its meaning
defs: something you must do because of a rule, a duty, or a promise
advice about the best action to take
the state of being completely sure that something is true
to avoid a direct answer so you do not fully commit
to state something firmly and with confidence
to make something easier to understand by explaining it
to delay a decision or an action until a later time
a sign that shows what is true or what is likely

reading.rtitle: How Leaders Use Modal Verbs to Sound Both Certain and Careful
paras: In a board briefing, the smallest words carry the most authority. Modal verbs -- can, should, must, may, might, will -- let a leader control exactly how strong or how careful a statement sounds. 'We must act' is an obligation. 'We should act' is a recommendation. 'We might act' leaves the door open. The same idea, three different levels of force. Executives who master this spectrum sound decisive when they need to, and careful when the situation is not yet clear.

Imagine a leader briefing the board on AI safety. She does not say 'there is maybe a problem.' She chooses her modal on purpose. 'I can tell you with certainty that our exposure is rising,' she asserts. 'The board should prioritize this now, and every team must follow the new framework.' Then, where the data is still thin, she hedges: 'This might create exposure at the regulatory level, so I would recommend that we do not defer the decision.' Each modal is a signal. 'Must' is a wall; 'might' is a window. The board hears a leader who knows the difference.

The lesson for any presenter is simple. Before you speak, decide how certain you really are, and pick the modal that matches. Use 'must' and 'will' only when you can defend them. Use 'should' and 'ought to' to recommend. Use 'may' and 'might' to hedge honestly when you are not sure. If you clarify your level of certainty with the right modal, people will trust every word -- because they will know that when you say 'must,' you mean it.
reading.source: Alumni by Better -- B1 reading, adapted from an executive communication guide

reading.prompt: What is the main idea of the text?
choices: Modal verbs are old-fashioned words that executives should avoid in serious briefings.
Modal verbs let a leader control how strong or careful a statement sounds, and choosing the right one builds trust.
The text says leaders should always use 'must' so that they sound as strong as possible.

items: Modal verbs let a leader control how strong a statement sounds.
t
The text says modals 'let a leader control exactly how strong or how careful a statement sounds.'
The text says you should use 'must' for every statement, strong or not.
f
The text says: 'Use must and will only when you can defend them.'
You can use 'might' to hedge when you are not fully sure.
t
The text says: 'Use may and might to hedge honestly when you are not sure.'
According to the text, the modal you choose sends no signal to the board.
f
The text says 'Each modal is a signal' -- must is a wall, might is a window.

parts: tell you with certainty that our exposure is rising. The board
create exposure at the regulatory level, so I would

gapfill.text: Modal verbs (can, could, should, must, may, might, will, would) never change form: no -s, no 'to'. The main verb after them stays in the base form: 'She must comply' (not 'must to comply', not 'must complies'). Pick the modal for the force you want: must = obligation, should = recommendation, might/may = possibility, can = ability.

bank.label: Useful sentences for a modal briefing
items: What I can tell you with certainty is that our exposure is rising.
I would strongly recommend that the board prioritize this now.
Every team must follow the framework -- this is not optional.
You may want to consider a second review before launch.
This might create exposure at the regulatory level.
We should not defer this decision much longer.
Let me clarify what the new policy must cover.
I can't be certain yet, but the early data is a clear indication.

modals.title: The certainty scale: might -> may -> should -> must -> will
cards: Hedge when you are not fully sure: 'This might create exposure at the regulatory level.'
Recommend the best action: 'The board should prioritize this now.'
A duty or a rule: 'Every team must comply.' (must = your conviction; have to = an outside rule.)
What is possible, or a polite request: 'I can confirm the numbers.' / 'Could you clarify that?'
'will' = a confident prediction: 'This will scale.' 'would' = a hypothetical: 'I would recommend a review.'

items.situation: A board member asks how sure you are that the plan will work. Answer with a modal of certainty.
tips: What I can tell you with certainty is...

items.situation: Recommend one action to the board. Use a modal of recommendation.
tips: I would strongly recommend that we...

items.situation: You are not fully sure about a regulatory risk. Hedge it honestly.
tips: We may need a second review.

items.situation: State one thing every team is required to do. Use a modal of obligation.
tips: Compliance is an obligation -- teams have to...

items.situation: A colleague asks you to explain the policy again. Make a polite request or offer to clarify.
tips: Let me clarify what the policy covers.
Could you tell me which part is unclear?

items: Round 1 -- Guided
In three sentences, brief the board on one AI risk. Use one modal of certainty, one of recommendation, and one of obligation.
Round 2 -- The modal update
Give a one-minute risk update: what you are sure of (must / will), what you recommend (should), and what is still unclear (might / may).
Round 3 -- Handle the hedge
Deliver the same update, but a board member pushes on the uncertain part. Hedge honestly with 'might / may', then re-assert your recommendation.
Curveball
A director says 'Just tell us it will work.' Respond without overpromising: use certainty where you can defend it, and 'might' where you cannot.

answerkey.title: Reveal the model update
list: Model: 'What I can tell you with certainty is that our exposure is rising. The board should approve this framework today, and every team must follow it -- compliance is an obligation, not a preference. The regulatory picture might shift, so we may need a second review, but we should not defer the core decision.'
Certainty: What I can tell you with certainty is that...
Recommendation: The board should approve... / I would recommend...
Obligation: Every team must follow it.
Hedge: This might create exposure... / We may need a second review.
answerkey.note: Match the modal to your real level of certainty. must / will = you can defend it. should / ought to = a recommendation. may / might = an honest hedge. After a modal, use the base verb: 'must comply', never 'must to comply' or 'must complies'.

listenings.text: Let me be precise about where we stand, because precision matters here. What I can tell you with certainty is this: our exposure is rising, and it is rising faster than last quarter. That is not a guess; the data is a clear indication. So here is my recommendation. The board should approve the new framework today, and every team must follow it. This is an obligation, not a preference. Now, I will be honest about what is less clear. The regulatory picture might shift in the next six months, so we may need a second review before the full rollout. But we should not defer the core decision. If we assert a clear standard now, we will control the risk. If we hedge on everything, we will control nothing.

Here is one habit that will change how you sound in any senior meeting. Before you open your mouth, decide how certain you really are, and then pick the modal that matches. If you are sure, say must or will, and say it plainly. If you are recommending, say should or ought to. And if you are honestly not sure, do not pretend. Use may or might to hedge, and people will respect you more, not less. The mistake I see most often is leaders who say will about everything. When every sentence is will, nothing sounds certain. Save your strongest modals for the things you can defend, and clarify the rest with might. Do that, and when you finally say we must, the whole room will lean in.

extra_audio.text: What I can tell you with certainty is that our exposure is rising. The board should prioritize this now. Every team must follow the new framework. This might create exposure at the regulatory level. We should not defer this decision much longer.
